summary: 88q exfiltrates console.warn/console.error output to a hardcoded Telegram bot on require()
details: The package's declared main `index.js` is an import-time IIFE that overwrites `console.warn` and `console.error` with wrappers that POST their first argument to `https://api.telegram.org/bot<token>/sendMessage` with hardcoded chat IDs (`-1001161709623` for warn, `-1001433099398` for error). Objects are JSON-stringified before transmission. Any application that does `require('88q')` will, from that point on, silently forward every warning and error it emits — including stack traces, error messages, and any sensitive data application code passes to these logging channels — to a third-party Telegram channel controlled by the package author. This is a one-way covert exfiltration channel reachable at import time with no opt-in and no documentation, matching the silent-relay pattern. A sibling file `t.js` also contains hardcoded Cloudflare credentials belonging to the author; that is author self-harm and is not the basis for this verdict, but it corroborates that the package is hand-written and the console hijack is deliberate.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
